prompt:
Hey, so I keep getting this error when I try to push and I have absolutely no idea what it means, git has always been kind of a mystery to me if I'm being honest. The message is:

```
error: failed to push some refs to 'github.com:acme/webapp.git'
hint: Updates were rejected because the remote contains work that you do not have locally.
```

Can you walk me through what happened here and what the safest way to fix it would be? I definitely don't want to lose anything my teammate pushed.

Given these tables:

orders(id, customer_id, created_at, total_cents)
customers(id, name, country)

Write a PostgreSQL query that returns the top 5 countries by total revenue in 2025, with revenue in whole dollars, sorted descending.

Okay so this is driving me a little crazy and I'm sure it's a classic beginner mistake, but here goes. I have a frontend running on http://localhost:5173 and an API on http://localhost:8000, and every fetch call fails in the browser with a CORS error, something about the Access-Control-Allow-Origin header being missing. The weird thing is the exact same request works perfectly fine when I do it from curl. Could you explain, like, what CORS is actually protecting against here, why curl doesn't care, and what the proper fix is on a FastAPI backend? I'd rather understand it than just paste a magic snippet, if that makes sense.

I need a regex and I'm terrible at regex, sorry in advance. Basically I have log lines and I want to pull out the timestamp and the request path. The lines look like this:

2026-07-01T14:22:09Z GET /api/v2/users/1841 200 12ms
2026-07-01T14:22:10Z POST /api/v2/orders 201 98ms

So I want two capture groups, one for the ISO timestamp at the start and one for the path that starts with a slash. It's for JavaScript if that matters. Could you also briefly explain each part of the pattern so I actually learn something this time?

So our Docker image for a small Node.js API somehow ended up at 1.4GB and the builds take around six minutes in CI, which feels completely absurd for what the service actually does. The Dockerfile is basically FROM node:20, COPY everything, npm install, npm run build, CMD node dist/server.js — you know, the naive version everyone writes first. I'd love it if you could walk me through the main things people do to get an image like this down to something reasonable, maybe multi-stage builds and better layer caching and whatever else applies, roughly in order of how much impact each change tends to have. We deploy maybe ten times a day so the CI time matters more than you'd think.

I could really use your advice on an architecture decision, and I promise I've done some homework before asking. We have a Next.js app and we need background jobs — sending emails, generating PDF invoices, that kind of thing, maybe a few hundred jobs a day at most, nothing crazy. Option one is spinning up a separate worker service with BullMQ and Redis, which feels like the 'proper' way but adds two new pieces of infrastructure we'd have to run and monitor. Option two is just using Vercel cron plus a database table as a poor man's queue, which feels slightly cursed but is basically zero new infra. Given the small scale, which would you actually pick, and what's the strongest argument against your own choice? Be honest, I don't need the diplomatic answer.
bash one-liner: 20 largest files under current dir, human-readable sizes
